ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dishes based on rice, with protein
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 has a generic biryani category (A0CKW represents composite rice dishes with protein). No specific code for 'egg biryani' was found, so 'closest_parent' is used to indicate a broader category match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for egg biryani as a prepared dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of egg biryani in Singapore is approximately 500 g, comprising a generous mound of spiced basmati rice topped with two to three hard-boiled eggs and accompanied by curry gravy or raita.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Biryani, rice with egg (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Singaporean egg biryani exists in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked basmati rice (FDC ID 169704), hard-boiled egg (FDC ID 173424), and ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 entry for egg biryani (per 100 g edible portion). The input record values (energy 150 kcal, protein 5 g, fat 6 g, carbohydrate 19 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 240 mg/100 g is plausible given added salt and spice blends. All values documented as adapted estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — Basmati rice, boiled (analogous estimate for egg biryani base)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for egg biryani as a composite dish. GI of 58 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice, boiled (GI ≈ 57–58, medium), which constitutes the dominant carbohydrate source (~70–75% of carbohydrate mass). The presence of yogurt, fat (ghee/oil), protein (egg), and spices is expected to modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain basmati rice. GI 58 is therefore a conservative upper-bound analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 95.0) / 100 = 55.1, where carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 19.0 × 5 = 95.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 240 mg/100 g with low potassium offset and low dietary fibre
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 g, request minimal added salt during cooking, avoid the optional curry gravy accompaniment (which adds significant additional sodium), and substitute ghee with unsaturated vegetable oil to reduce cardiovascular co-risk; estimated potassium is insufficient to offset borderline sodium at this serving size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI basmati rice base (GI ≈ 58) with 19 g carbohydrate/100 g and low dietary fibre (1.2 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion size to 150–200 g to limit total carbohydrate load per meal, pair with raita (plain yogurt with cucumber) to slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose response, and avoid the optional curry gravy if it contains added sugar or refined starch; the glycemic load of a full 500 g plate (~55 GL units) is high and incompatible with tight glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 76 mg/100 g from hard-boiled eggs, saturated fat 2.4 g/100 g, and sodium 240 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit egg biryani to small portions (150–200 g) no more than twice weekly, request substitution of ghee with a polyunsaturated vegetable oil to reduce saturated fat intake, and avoid the curry gravy accompaniment to keep sodium within the 1500–2000 mg/day target; the anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, ginger, cinnamon) provides modest cardioprotective benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, ginger, cinnamon, cloves, cardamom) with saturated fat well below 5 g/100 g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Egg biryani is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory spice content; however, those sensitive to nightshades (tomato, chilli) should monitor individual tolerance, and ghee should be substituted with vegetable oil to minimise saturated fat contribution.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg Biryani (also spelled Egg Briyani or Egg Biriyani) is a fragrant one-pot rice dish popular across Singapore, Malaysia, and South Asia. It is prepared by cooking long-grain basmati rice with a richly spiced base of sautéed onions, ginger, garlic, and tomatoes, seasoned with a blend of whole and ground biryani spices including turmeric, cumin, coriander, cardamom, cinnamon, and cloves. Hard-boiled eggs are layered into the rice and the dish is simmered or dum-cooked until the rice is fluffy and fully infused with the aromatic spice mixture. The result is a golden-hued, mildly spiced rice with a tender, slightly firm egg, offering a savoury, earthy, and warmly spiced flavour profile with a soft, fluffy texture. In Singapore, egg biryani is a staple halal dish served at Indian-Muslim restaurants, hawker centres, and food delivery platforms, typically accompanied by a side of curry gravy or cooling raita. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 150 kcal, 5 g protein, 6 g fat (2.4 g saturated), 19 g carbohydrate, 1.2 g dietary fibre, and 240 mg sodium. The medium glycemic index (~58) from basmati rice means portion control is important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. The anti-inflammatory spice profile makes it a reasonable choice for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis when prepared with vegetable oil rather than ghee.
common_user_queries: How many calories in egg biryani?
Is egg biryani suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of egg biryani?
How much protein in egg biryani?
Can I eat egg biryani if I have high blood pressure?
What spices are in egg biryani?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Singaporean egg biryani exists in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a named composite dish. Nutrient values are cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked basmati rice and hard-boiled egg, and ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for egg biryani. The input values are internally consistent and plausible. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database basmati rice data. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for egg biryani as a composite dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN tables at the level of individual fatty acid fractions for this preparation. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not directly reported in the consulted approved sources; estimated ~185 mg/100 g referenced in the input health suitability notes is not from an approved primary source and is therefore not carried forward as a numeric value. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values for egg biryani as a composite Singaporean dish are not available in the approved sources consulted (USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN 2017, HPB Foodo) at the required specificity. 4. No core macro (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) is zero; zero-macro justification rule is not triggered. 5. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.1 g/100 g from the input record; this is a trace level consistent with ghee or partially hydrogenated oil use and is retained. 6. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03311069 is the closest defensible match for biryani as a food product class; FoodEx2 code A0CKW is used as a 'closest_parent' match for composite rice dishes with protein — no egg-biryani-specific FoodEx2 code was identified. 7. GI of 58 is a conservative analogous estimate based on basmati rice (University of Sydney GI Database); actual GI of the composite dish with egg, fat, and yogurt may be modestly lower. 8. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as HPB Singapore but does not resolve to an HPB nutrient composition table; it has been replaced with the USDA FoodData Central as the primary approved source for nutrient cross-validation.
